Welcome, everyone, to the first episode of Technique Tuesday, where every week we're going to take an in-depth look at the lost art and science of training technique. Just as a quick general outline, for the most part, we will break each exercise into four sections. We will look at the muscles we'll be targeting, how to set up for the exercise, the execution of the movement, and common errors that I see many people make. So without further ado, let's jump right into it with the bench press exercise.

The muscles we'll be targeting here, in order of involvement, are the pec major, both the sternal (mid and lower) and clavicular (upper) heads, the triceps brachii, the anterior (front) deltoid, and, to a lesser extent, the lats. They'll only be active when the shoulder is behind the torso, at the very bottom of the press. The primary actions being trained are horizontal shoulder adduction, carried out mainly by the pecs and anterior deltoids, some degree of shoulder flexion by the upper pecs and front delt, and elbow extension, primarily handled by the triceps.

Before we get into the full setup, it's important to emphasize that you should only compare your bench strength to your starting place and not to anyone else's. Many extraneous factors can contribute to performance on this lift, like arm length, rib cage size, muscle origins and insertions, lumbar spine mobility, and the size and strength of your upper back for creating a stable base of support.

The setup for the bench press is extremely important, and you may want to play this back a couple of times. I'll go through a strength-focused powerlifting-style bench press, which is a great mass builder for anyone aiming to build size. However, I'll explain slight modifications you can make if you have purely bodybuilding interests.

Here we go. First, double-check that you have the right weight on both sides, that the bar is centered in the rack, and that you have a spotter handy if you're training at high efforts. Lie flat on the bench with your head back behind the bar. With your feet up on the bench, squeeze your shoulder blades together, keeping them in this retracted position throughout the setup and execution of the lift. Setup your arch by lifting your arms up and grabbing the bar with a shoulder-width grip, or you can reach back and push against the bench's uprights. I prefer just grabbing the bar. Lift your hips high, pulling your shoulder blades to your butt. This helps force you into scapular depression and pushes your upper body forward, keeping your feet in place. You can go up onto your toes if you find you can get more arch that way.

Now, your back should be planted on the bench, with your eyes directly under the barbell. Position your lower body for leg drive by lowering one foot at a time, positioning your feet as far back as you comfortably can, and keeping your legs close to the bench when viewed from the front. If using regulation technique, your heels should be planted on the ground, so you may need to externally rotate your feet or point your toes out. Make sure your hips are down so your butt touches the bench. Take your grip at this point. I widen my grip, which is on the wider side, as I'm stronger with a wider grip.

Research has found that wider grips will target the sternal head of the pecs and anterior delts more, while a closer grip will target the triceps and clavicular (upper) chest more. Using a variety of grip widths and experimenting to find what works best for you makes sense. However, an important factor in determining grip width is whether your joints are stacked from the rear position, meaning the wrist should be roughly positioned over the elbows. It's worth noting advanced powerlifters may not always keep the joints perfectly stacked. A 2007 study suggests a max width of 1.5 times the acromial distance (roughly 1.5 times shoulder-width) can reduce injury risk without seriously compromising maximal strength.

Another important point regarding grip is ensuring your wrist joints are stacked. From the side view, your wrist should be directly below your knuckles, avoiding excessive hyperextension. You can correct this by thinking about punching the ceiling as a cue. Before the lift-off, do one last check to make sure your shoulder blades are retracted and depressed by cranking your elbows forward, which I think of as screwing my back into position.

For those interested in bodybuilding only, the general setup can still apply. You won't need as extreme of an arch, and may want to take a slightly more narrow grip to increase the range of motion. Because your arch will be smaller, your feet can be a bit further forward, but still, for shoulder health and stability, keep your shoulder blades retracted and maintain some arch in your lumbar spine. People tend to exaggerate the difference in range of motion between a big arch and a small arch. It's worth noting that the distance traveled by the bar isn't necessarily the same as joint range of motion. For example, compare the joint angles in these two bench press styles. The position of the humerus relative to the torso at the end of the range of motion is similar despite the bar traveling further in the flat back press. You should work on building your arch and not worry too much about limiting your range of motion when using it.

We've got the bench press setup down; now it's time to execute the set. We'll break the execution into four phases: unrack, brace, descend, and press. First, unrack the bar with your spotter's help, lifting out, not up, or, if unracking by yourself, keep your butt elevated for the liftoff component, then drop your hips once you've unracked. I prefer having a spotter near a maximal effort to help with the liftoff. Ensure you have four main points of contact: your head, upper back, glutes, and feet should all be planted. In position, take a deep breath into your gut, pressing the air out against your belt if you have one. You can cue yourself to puff your chest out to expand the rib cage as much as possible. Grip the bar as hard as you can. You can optionally use the cue to bend the bar or rip it in half, activating your upper back muscles to maintain tightness. During the descent, drop your elbows at a 45-degree angle relative to your torso when viewed from the top. There's unanimous

rip it in half, activating your upper back muscles to maintain tightness. During the descent, drop your elbows at a 45-degree angle relative to your torso when viewed from the top. There's unanimous agreement on this from the best powerlifting coaches. When viewed from the side, the elbows shouldn't be too far in front of the bar. Aim to keep the elbow stacked under the bar. The descending bar path should be down and slightly forward. Touch your chest just below nipple level, toward the bottom of your sternum, which should be the highest contact point on your chest. You can optionally pause on your chest for about a second until the bar becomes motionless, which is required for competitive powerlifting. For bodybuilding, simply tap your chest without a pause. Ensure the touch is well controlled and not bouncing the weight off your chest.

After your optional pause, explode the bar off your chest, driving your heels into the floor. Think about pressing the bar back toward your face and off your chest, rather than just driving the bar straight up. Think about pushing the floor away from you with your feet to help initiate leg drive and enforce this back-and-up bar path. Don't press the bar too far back; it shouldn't hit the rack or cause you to lose control. Bring it back into balance over the shoulder joint, restoring the starting position. On the way up, you might encounter a sticking point if training at high efforts where the lift feels most difficult. It's likely where you'll fail the lift if going for a max effort set. Research shows it tends to be in the first 20 to 40% of the movement, about 3 to 4 inches off the chest. It'll be individual; typically, the more advanced you are, the lower the sticking point, and the newer you are, the more it'll be in the mid-range. To break through the sticking point, ensure

chest. It'll be individual; typically, the more advanced you are, the lower the sticking point, and the newer you are, the more it'll be in the mid-range. To break through the sticking point, ensure you're pressing the bar up and back, not just straight up. You can try flaring the elbows out a bit more to get your pecs to help the triceps. Think about lifting the bar off your chest with max speed to blast through the point where deceleration would normally happen. The simplest solution is likely you need stronger pecs and triceps.

Once you're accelerating past the sticking point, you can opt to exhale, but you can hold your breath until you fully lock the lift out. As you near the end range of motion, the bar should complete the arc back toward the starting position over the nipples. Repeat the process, starting with bracing, descending, and pressing again for however many reps your program calls for.

Three common errors I see all the time with the bench press are first, over-tucking or under-tucking the elbows. The cue to tuck your elbows originated in equipped powerlifting, but without a bench shirt, it may not be helpful for you. It's just as common to see people not tucking enough, flaring their elbows out parallel with the bar. This is also not ideal; it puts your shoulder at an increased risk of injury and makes the bar path less efficient since it's forced to go straight up and down. Most won't be as strong benching with flared elbows. A slight tuck is good. As you get to the sticking point, more of a flair might help you finish the lift. If you have difficulty figuring out how you're using your elbows, I'd recommend slow eccentric presses with lighter weights to be mindful of where your elbows are and where they're going.

The second common error is benching with a completely flat back. You may not need as extreme of an arch as a competitive powerlifter, but maintaining some arch confers at least three major benefits: first, the lumbar and thoracic vertebrae and intervertebral discs are safer in the lordotic or arched position; the more decline pushing angle is safer for the shoulder joint; and you'll likely be stronger in this position, meaning more total load on the active muscle groups.

The third error is a lack of leg drive. If your feet are positioned wherever, just out in front of you on the bench rather than tucked behind you, you're not maximizing your strength potential. Figuring out how to use your legs in the bench press may take time, but mastering it is important. I've linked a few good articles in the description.

Okay, welcome everyone to a new episode of Technique Tuesday. This week, we're going to be looking at how to perform the squat with perfect technique. The back squat is often referred to as the king of lower body exercises and even the king of all exercises, and that's an appointment I think it actually deserves. Before we jump into the technique for this exercise, let's take a look at what muscles we're going to be targeting with this movement first.

So with the squat, we're training simultaneous hip extension, where the hips straighten out on the way up, and knee extension, where the knees straighten out. The hip extension component will be handled primarily by the glutes, getting some assistance from the adductor magnus. And even though the hamstrings can perform hip extension, they actually don't contribute much to the squat at all because of the bent knee, meaning their muscle length doesn't change much throughout the range of motion. The knee extension component will be handled by the quadriceps, and we'll be performing an isometric lumbar extension contraction, which will be handled by the spinal erectors of the lower back.

Now another very common misconception is that the abs are highly active in the squat, but that simply isn't true. EMG data shows low abdominal activity in the squat, which makes sense given that their function is spinal flexion and anti-extension, neither of which occur in the squat. So we're mainly looking at the glutes, quads, and spinal erectors as the muscular targets of the squat. And because the squat involves multiple joints and large muscle masses, it's very conducive to progressive overload and should be trained with relatively heavy weight in a low to moderate rep zone.

While there are exceptions, doing squats for greater than 10 reps or so will usually cause your cardiovascular system to fatigue before your leg musculature, leading to sloppy form, worse strength gains, and lower muscle activation. Now generally, I recommend loading the squat with at least seventy percent of your one-rep max or in the five to ten rep zone if your goal is primarily hypertrophy, and the three to six rep zone if your goal is primarily strength. And again, there will be exceptions to this. I know it's important to be very objective about where your form stands and possibly consider lightening the weight on the bar for a few months while you master the technique, and then you can build your way back up to your previous loads with good form. A few months of not going as heavy as possible will be far better than taking a few months off due to an injury from lifting beyond your capability.

Before approaching the bar, do a quick check to make sure you have the right weight on both sides and that the bar is centered in the rack. You want to position the bar height so that when you unrack the bar you aren't hitting the j-hooks during the walkout, and for most bar positions around armpit height will be a good height. For reasons I've discussed in another video, which I'll link below, I think people actually exaggerate the practical difference between a high bar squat with the bar resting up on your traps and a low bar squat with the bar resting down on your rear delts. Research shows that ultimately the high bar squat may be a bit more quad dominant and the low bar squat may be a bit more hip dominant. Now in my experience, I think the high bar squat tends to protect the lower back more and tends to feel a bit more comfortable for many people, so we'll be squatting with the high bar position here.

Now, the bar is too high if it's pushing your head forward, and it's too low if it feels like it's going to slide down your back. You want to position your hands as comfortably close to your shoulders as possible, as a closer grip here will activate the muscles of the upper back better, leading to a more stable base of support for the bar. Your hands should merely be stabilizing the bar, keeping it mashed between your palms and your upper back, so you shouldn't be actively gripping the bar like you would in a bench press, and just let it really sit and settle in there on your upper back.

So with both feet underneath the bar, you want to push your hips forward to unrack the bar, and don't make the common mistake of squatting the bar up with your knees as this will cause unnecessary quad fatigue before the actual lift itself and can contribute to knee pain. You want to take three steps backward: one small step with your non-dominant leg, one medium step with the other leg, and then a third mini step to get your feet even. Once your feet are set, they should be planted with about a 15 to 30 degree foot flare; in general, you just want to make sure that your feet are at least wider than shoulder-width.

Now that you've got the squat set up, it's time to actually execute the lift, and we're going to split this up into three distinct phases: brace, descend, and lift. So for the bracing phase, your heel and big toe should be making even pressure on the ground, and your weight should be centered on the middle of your foot. I don't really like the cue to keep your weight on your heels since it can throw your balance off and cause your hips to shoot up early. You really want to have your weight evenly distributed across the entire foot. Your head should be slightly extended with your eyes gazing straight ahead, and your elbows should be pointing slightly back, so try not to position them directly under the bar, which can lead to elbow pain. You want to pull a huge breath of air deep into your diaphragm, pushing your gut out against your belt to create a solid, rigid column down your spine. Now you're fully braced and ready to begin the descent.

Now for the high bar position, you want to break at the hips and the knees at the same time. Just looking at the knees first here, you want to push your knees forward and slightly out so that they track in the same direction as your toes, while at the same time sitting your hips straight down. You should be pushing through your heel, big toe, and pinky toe throughout the eccentric, keeping an even weight distribution through your feet. And when viewed from the side, the bar path should be a perfectly straight line moving straight down, centered directly over the middle of the foot. You want to lower the bar under control, actively driving your upper back into the bar and not allowing your chest to fall forward. You don't want to descend excessively slowly to the point where you start to lose gas too early. Generally, you only want about a one-second negative on the squat.

Now when it comes to squat depth, I recommend at least squatting parallel to the ground as a minimum standard for hypertrophy and general strength. However, for regulation technique, you technically do need to get the hip crease below the knee joint, meaning you will have to go below parallel, and I think most people should be able to squat to this depth when using a high bar position and with the appropriate stance width for particular skeleton and mobility. So this position is what we call being in the hole. Unless you're doing paused squats as a specific training drill, you generally don't want to pause in the hole at all but rather take advantage of the stretch reflex at the bottom and then immediately reverse the motion the second you hit depth. And although you do want to maintain an erect upright posture during the positive, I'm not a big fan of the chest up cue myself since it can cause some trainees to lean too far backward. I prefer to think about driving your back up into

an erect upright posture during the positive, I'm not a big fan of the chest up cue myself since it can cause some trainees to lean too far backward. I prefer to think about driving your back up into the bar while keeping your head slightly up, which is gonna pull the spine into a good lifting position.

Now remember that with the squat, we're trying to move heavy weight and maximize our strength potential, so don't worry about squeezing any muscles or feeling the burn or anything like that, and you want to explode out of the hole as if you were trying to jump through the ceiling. I personally keep my breath held until about two-thirds of the way up, at which point I exhale, and once I'm back up to the top, I'll begin the bracing phase again, take another deep breath in, and then repeat for however many reps my program calls for.

Now because there are so many errors with the squat, I may have to just do another video on just the mistakes alone. I did actually do one myth bust video explaining why you should actually let your knees come forward past the toes and why the cue to keep your knees behind the toes can actually be harmful to the lower back, and I'll link that in the description below. Now, other than that, I would say one really common error that I see is knee valgus, or having the knees cave in either on the negative or the positive. This usually stems from lack of proper hip external rotation, so you want to make sure your feet are adequately flared out, which will help. Then you want to actively think about driving your knees out in the direction of your toes, but not excessively beyond the direction of your toes, and simply placing a mini band or hip circle around your knees can help to cue for this and strengthen the hip abductors. And wearing a heeled squat shoe can also help. Excessive foot

of your toes, and simply placing a mini band or hip circle around your knees can help to cue for this and strengthen the hip abductors. And wearing a heeled squat shoe can also help. Excessive foot pronation or losing your arch is a known contributor to knee valgus.

The other two common errors usually come as a two-for-one deal here: going too heavy and not going deep enough, because the squat, unlike say the deadlift, starts with the eccentric phase, so it's much easier to cheat by loading up the bar with more weight than you can handle and then only going halfway down. Whereas on the deadlift if you can't lift the weight, then it just stays where it is on the ground, so it's a little bit harder to cheat on that one.

Now you also want to be careful with butt wink or lower back rounding here, and if your back is excessively rounding on the squat, you may want to start cutting your depth a bit shorter or just modify your stance width either more or less wide. But I think that some degree of butt wink is basically an inevitability for many lifters and isn't a cause for too much concern, especially if it's mild and isn't causing any pain or discomfort. Foam rolling and dynamic stretching before squatting will also increase joint range of motion acutely and may help reduce butt wink as you hit depth.

Lunges are great as well but won't have that same overloading potential as the squat will. And as a last resort, I'd say a leg press plus a lower back extension captures a decent amount of what the squat is training.

When you're learning how to bench press, it might be prudent to use a spotter if one is available. However, if you're working inside a correctly set up power rack, a spotter is not absolutely necessary. Start with an empty bar. Lie down on the bench with your eyes looking straight up. In this position, you should be far enough down from the bar—meaning toward the foot end of the bench—that when you look up, your eyes are focused on the downside of the bar. Your feet should be flat on the ground with your shins approximately vertical. Your upper back should be flat against the bench with your lower back in an anatomically normal arched position. Take an overhand grip on the bar; your grip should be somewhere between 22 and 24 inches measured between the index fingers. This grip width will result in a vertical forearm for most people when the bar is touching the chest at the bottom. The bar should rest on the heel of your palm, not on the fingers, and directly over the bones of the

result in a vertical forearm for most people when the bar is touching the chest at the bottom. The bar should rest on the heel of your palm, not on the fingers, and directly over the bones of the forearm. Just like the press, this grip is best accomplished by pronating your hands with a slight internal rotation of your arms. You are now ready to take the bar out of the rack. Look directly up at the ceiling and push up on the bar, locking out your elbows. Move the bar out to a position directly over the line of your shoulder joints. This is the place where the bar is in balance at lockout. Note carefully the position of the bar against the ceiling; this picture is your reference for the path the bar will take as you move it down and up. You will lower the bar to your chest, touch the chest, and then drive the bar right back to the exact position you are looking at on the ceiling overhead. Stare at the place on the ceiling where the bar is to go; do not watch the bar as it moves. With

and then drive the bar right back to the exact position you are looking at on the ceiling overhead. Stare at the place on the ceiling where the bar is to go; do not watch the bar as it moves. With the bar locked out over the shoulders, have your spotter touch your chest a few inches below the bar's vertical position, at about the middle of your sternum. This tactile cue will quite effectively identify the point on your chest to touch the bar. If there is no spotter and you're benching alone inside a rack, unlock your elbows and then allow them to drop towards your feet a little on the way down. If you do this correctly, the net effect will be the same as the spotter's touch. The bar will contact your sternum a few inches below your clavicles and therefore below your shoulder joints. Look at the ceiling, unlock your elbows, lower the bar to your chest, touch without stopping, and then drive the bar back at the point on the ceiling your eyes have trapped. Try it for a set of five reps.

at the ceiling, unlock your elbows, lower the bar to your chest, touch without stopping, and then drive the bar back at the point on the ceiling your eyes have trapped. Try it for a set of five reps. You'll immediately notice that if your eyes don't move from their fixed position, the bar will go to the same place every rep. Do another set of five with the bar, reinforcing your eye position, and then rack the bar. This is done with locked elbows after the last rep is finished. Should you have a spotter, this movement back to the rack should be covered. Now that we've covered the basic movement pattern for the bench press, we can maximize the efficiency of the lift by improving the position of the upper back, legs, and feet. The upper back needs to be planted firmly against the bench and used as a platform to drive against while the arms drive the bar up. When this is done correctly, the shoulder blades will be adducted or pinched together. Sit on the bench in the same position you

used as a platform to drive against while the arms drive the bar up. When this is done correctly, the shoulder blades will be adducted or pinched together. Sit on the bench in the same position you assumed before lying down to take the bar. Imagine a hand touching you right between the shoulder blades and imagine pinching the hand between your shoulder blades. This pinching will also cause you to raise your chest, further contributing to a good position. Now lie down, take the bar out, and assume this same position, making sure your shoulder blades are pinched together and your chest is up high. Your goal is to be able to do all your reps without losing this set position against the bench. Used correctly, the legs drive against the floor during the bench press, transferring force horizontally along the bench through the hips into the arched back to reinforce the arch and keep the chest in its high position. Remember that you cannot raise your butt up off of the bench at any time

along the bench through the hips into the arched back to reinforce the arch and keep the chest in its high position. Remember that you cannot raise your butt up off of the bench at any time during the lift. Your foot position should be set so that your shins are nearly vertical. This position allows for the efficient use of your legs in reinforcing the arch, but it doesn't predispose you to bridge your butt up off of the bench. The proper position for the feet is flat against the floor so that the heels can be used as the base of the drive up the legs. If you're up on your toes, you cannot use the force of knee extension nearly as efficiently as you can if your heels are planted. For the next sets of five reps, add a little weight until the bar speed begins to slow down and your form starts to change. Stay at that weight for two more sets of five, and this is the first work set.

Research is my passion. Muscle and strength are my pursuits. I'm the powerlifter, bodybuilder, scientist, and coach. I'm Layne Norton. I am a physique architect. The bench press is one of the most important upper body exercises. This makes it crucial for overall development and strength. A lot of people think about it as just a chest movement, but it incorporates the pectorals, the triceps, the shoulders, and even the back when done correctly. When it's done wrong, the results can be disastrous. Seven years ago, I tore my right pectoral bench pressing incorrectly. But over time, I learned to incorporate more muscle groups and focus on the movement itself. Not only did it become more safe, but my results were better. In this video, I'm going to teach you things I learned from top technique experts like Ben Escrow, Dr. Mike Zourdos, and Matt Gary to make my bench press more effective, safe, and fun.

Before we actually get into talking about the nuts and bolts, I want to talk about something a lot of people don't really think about—that's equipment. Now, you don't need any equipment other than yourself to bench press, but there are certain things that can make your bench press more effective and safe. Not many people give much thought to footwear on a bench press, but your feet are what connect you to the ground and form your base. Now, footwear is a little bit of personal preference, but you want something with a lot of grip. Personally, I like to use squat shoes. They have an elevated heel, which helps me get my foot flat on the floor and connect with it better. They also have really good grip on the bottom, and that helps keep my feet from sliding. But many people also use wrestling shoes and shoes like Chuck Taylors.

Another piece that really isn't equipment but I find to be very useful is chalk. By putting chalk on my back, it helps me stick to the bench better and form a more stable base to press from. The easiest way to apply chalk is to use a partner or lifting buddy. Have them chalk your back where your back contacts the bench. The most important piece of equipment I use on the bench press is wrist straps. If you're going to create the maximum amount of force, you want to be able to keep your wrist in a straight line. Wrist straps give joint support, helping you keep your wrist in a straight line. I recommend a good pair of powerlifting wrist straps anywhere from 12 to 36 inches, depending on what you prefer. Many people make the mistake of wrapping their wrists too low. You want the wrap to actually cover the joint. In fact, you want to wrap a little bit above and a little bit below the joint. This is why I prefer a bit longer wrist wrap of eighteen to twenty-four inches. This ensures I can

cover the joint. In fact, you want to wrap a little bit above and a little bit below the joint. This is why I prefer a bit longer wrist wrap of eighteen to twenty-four inches. This ensures I can get enough revolutions to cover both above and below the wrist joint. Tightness is a little bit of a matter of personal preference. I say get them as tight as you can while not causing pain.

Now that we've covered equipment, let's talk about what actually makes the components of a good bench press. The first thing I want to talk about is foot placement. While not as crucial as on squat or deadlift, it still is important on the bench press. It forms your connection to the ground and your base. I prefer to keep a flat foot on the ground. This ensures I get the best connection possible. I also like to keep my back tight and arched. To do that, I like to keep my feet back as far as I can while still keeping them flat. For everyone, this is going to be a different position, but you want to put them back, but not so far back that you can't keep them flat on the floor.

The next thing I want to talk about is back position. As you lie down on the bench, you want to position your back in the appropriate position. For everyone, this may be a little bit different, but essentially, what you want is to have your back in a position where you're not so far away from the bar that it becomes difficult to unrack, but you're not so far under it that you're likely to hit the pegs when you're pressing. I prefer to put the bar right around forehead height, and this will put my back connection with the bench just below where the bar sits on the rack.

So now that we're in the proper position, I want to talk about back arch. Now, this tends to be a little bit of a controversial topic, especially amongst bodybuilders. Many bodybuilders don't think they should arch; they think that an arch is just a powerlifting move. But arching your lower back will help keep your spine neutral and protect it while you're pressing. It'll also ensure your overall back stays tighter. Now, if you're not concerned about powerlifting, maybe you don't need to arch as much as powerlifters, but you should still keep a slight arch in your lower back. If you're a powerlifter, then you want to arch as much as you possibly can to minimize the distance the bar has to travel.

The next thing I want to talk about is gripping the bar. You want to grab it tightly and with authority. It's going to activate your central nervous system and get you ready and cue you to stay tight. You want to put the bar as far down in your hand as you possibly can. If you hold the bar out in your fingers, it is more likely to cause your wrist to roll back. This means you are no longer pressing with optimal force. A straight wrist will be the most optimal way to press, so you want to hold the bar as low in your hands as you can.

Grip width is going to be very individual, depending on your arm length. People with longer arms will probably need to grip wider. People with shorter arms can grip more narrow, but I don't like exaggerated grips in either direction. Most people are going to be around the rings or just a few inches inside the rings. I also don't recommend a false or thumbless grip. While they may feel better, they are simply more dangerous. I've heard numerous stories of people dropping bars on their chest because they use the thumbless grip. Wrap your thumb around the bar and bench press more safely.

The next thing I want to talk about is squeezing your shoulder blades together. Squeezing your shoulder blades together will allow you to stay tighter. It also protects the shoulders. What I mean by squeezing shoulder blades together is to think about if you had a grape between your shoulder blades—you want to squeeze so tight that you crush that grape. Just like on squat, we want to breathe to create the maximum amount of intra-abdominal pressure so we can brace more effectively and move more weight. Before you begin the movement, you want to take in a deep breath, unrack the bar, blow that breath out, and take in another new deep breath before you start your first rep. Then you want to hold it and blow out forcefully as you lock out the weight. This will enable you to use more weight more effectively.

One step that gets overlooked is unracking the bar. Just like on squat, we want to do this as efficiently as possible and not waste energy. The way I like to think about unracking the bar is not to lift the bar off the uprights, but I want to think about driving my back against the bench so hard that the bar just comes off. You want to take a deep breath, brace, drive your shoulder blades against the bench, and then slowly and in a controlled fashion pull the bar off the uprights.

Now we've gone through a proper setup on the bench press. Let's talk execution. After you've unracked the bar and you're ready to start your first rep, you want to take in a big breath and brace your abdominal wall, just like you would on squat or deadlift. Once you're braced, to initiate lowering the bar, think about trying to bend the bar into a U. This will enable the bar to lower naturally in a good position with your elbows tucked. By thinking about bending the bar, you're going to naturally engage your lats and tuck your elbows. We don't want to flare our elbows excessively on the bench press. Tucking your elbows ensures that you'll engage your lats and press in a more safe and efficient manner. Another cue that may work is to protect your armpits.

Where you touch the bar on your body is very important and is going to be very different depending on how long your arms are and where you grip. One of the most important things for an efficient bench press is to keep your forearm at 90 degrees to the ground. If it moves in either direction, you're going to be losing energy transfer as you press up with the bar. Somebody who has long arms and grips narrow is going to have to touch very low on their body. If somebody has very short arms and grips very wide, they could touch very high and still keep the forearm at 90 degrees to the ground. But as we discussed, I don't recommend extreme grips in either direction. For most people, they're going to hit anywhere from their top ab to their nipple line.

Once you've touched the bar to your chest, you want to initiate the upward movement by tightening your glutes and driving your legs into the ground. This may seem like a strange cue, but just like on squat and deadlift, using leg drive is going to enable you to use more weight and stay tighter. Now, on the bench press, because our feet are not directly under the bar, it's not as important as it is on squats and deadlifts. But we're still trying to get every little bit of efficiency that we can out of our movement. As you start to press up, you want to think about throwing the bar back as you start the lockout process. Remember, we're pressing the bar in an upside-down J path. Ideally, we'd have a completely straight bar path, but with most people on the bench press, that's not going to be possible. If they do, the bar ends up too far away from you as you lock out and creates extra torque on your shoulders.

Now that we've talked about how to do a proper bench press, let's talk about some of the most common mistakes I see when people do the movement. Probably the most common mistake I see is people bouncing the bar off their chest. Not only is this dangerous because of the force you create on your sternum, but it's almost impossible to stay tight and controlled when you're bouncing the bar off your chest. And if you're bouncing it, are you really moving the weight? Another mistake is people don't breathe properly and brace properly. If you're not taking a big breath before you start and bracing your abdominal wall correctly, it's going to be less safe, and you're not going to be able to stay as tight and use as much weight.

Another one of the most common mistakes I see is people flaring their elbows. Many people flare their elbows out because they believe this will lead to more chest recruitment. And even if it does, it's still not worth the danger. This was how I tore my pec seven years ago, by using too wide of a grip and flaring my elbows out. It also means you're not going to be able to keep your lats tight, and it's going to lead to less efficiency in the movement.

Another big mistake I see is rolling your shoulders forward. This tends to go hand in hand with flaring elbows out too wide. Many people tend to roll their shoulders forward as they finish the movement, but this is going to make you stay less tight and has a greater chance of leading to some sort of shoulder injury over the course of your lifting career. Another mistake that coincides with flaring elbows and rolling shoulders is unlocking your shoulder blades. Many people will unlock their shoulder blades as they finish a bench press because they feel it will enable them to lock out more weight, but that's not the case. It will cause you to lose tightness and increase the possibility of injury.

Another mistake I see is people not keeping their feet planted. They have what I call floppy feet. It usually coincides with all the other cues we've talked about of not staying tight. Keep your feet planted on the floor. This will enable you to stay tighter and press more effectively. Now you have the tools to bench press effectively and safely. Remember, lifting weights is a skill, just like any other sport, and the more times you do it, the better you will get at that skill. Start out with light weight, work on your form, and build it up over time. Now, I know it's a lot to remember. There's a lot of cues when it comes to bench pressing, so go back and watch this video over and over until it makes more sense. You may pick up more things every time you watch it. Also, I've broken everything down in detail in this article at bodybuilding.com. For more articles and videos from me and other great contributors, keep coming back to bodybuilding.com.

What’s going on, Nation? I’m Scott from MuscularStrength.com, and today we’re going to go over the three golden rules for performing a barbell incline bench press. But before we get started, if you’ve been enjoying my Golden Rule series, be sure to smash that like and subscribe button, and let me know which exercise you want to see next down in the comment section below. Alright, guys, golden rule number one: you always want to make sure you’re benching with a grip just outside shoulder width. I know some of you are going to say that with a wider grip you can bench more weight, but when you have a wider grip on this exercise using a barbell, you’re not going to get much of a squeeze at the top of the movement. It should go without saying: the more you bring your hands in, the more of a squeeze you get in your chest—adduction is one of the functions of the chest—which means if you’re feeling that squeeze at the top, you’re going to get more muscle activation, more muscle damage, and

squeeze you get in your chest—adduction is one of the functions of the chest—which means if you’re feeling that squeeze at the top, you’re going to get more muscle activation, more muscle damage, and more growth, even if you can bench a little more with a wider grip. With a just-outside-shoulder-width grip, you’re also going to have more control over the exercise, and you’ll be able to keep your elbows under your wrists, allowing you to really focus and push through with your triceps as well. Let me show you what I mean. Getting underneath the barbell, place your fingertips on the knurling—I like to use my thumbs, put them right on the end—grab the barbell, lift off, and then as I perform my repetitions, like I just told you guys, I keep my elbows under my wrists, and as I press back up, my elbows stay under my wrists as well. Make sure that as you come down, you don’t flare your elbows out to the side and then push that way. You’re going to lose a lot of power if you do that, and

my elbows stay under my wrists as well. Make sure that as you come down, you don’t flare your elbows out to the side and then push that way. You’re going to lose a lot of power if you do that, and you’re also going to place a lot of unwanted tension on your shoulders. So, golden rule number one: get that just-outside-shoulder-width grip. Golden rule number two is having your shoulder blades retracted and depressed throughout the entire set. The most common thing I notice is many of you will retract, depress, lean back, and get into position, but sometimes the J-hooks are too high on benches in the gym, or in general when we try to press the weight off the J-hooks, we lift the weight and then push it up like this, almost using our shoulders to help us. Once you use your shoulders to help you, you unpack them, and then you start performing your repetitions with your back flat on the bench versus having your shoulder blades retracted and depressed. What starts to happen is you lose a lot

you unpack them, and then you start performing your repetitions with your back flat on the bench versus having your shoulder blades retracted and depressed. What starts to happen is you lose a lot of that pushing power, and you also start to transfer more of the exercise into your shoulders versus keeping it all in your chest. If that happens to you, hold the weight above you and reset your shoulders. Once your shoulders are nice and packed, you’ll be able to bench and feel it all in your chest and not in your shoulders at all. You probably saw a clear difference when I benched with my shoulders packed versus with my back flat on the bench. There’s a big difference. The third golden rule is to remember that your lower body and your core are vital to this movement. Yes, it’s an upper body movement—we’re pressing through our chest and our arms—but if your core is loose and your feet are not firmly planted on the ground, then you don’t have a solid foundation to press more weight

body movement—we’re pressing through our chest and our arms—but if your core is loose and your feet are not firmly planted on the ground, then you don’t have a solid foundation to press more weight comfortably. When you’re pressing the weight and lift it off the rack—shoulders packed, bar lifted—you don’t want your feet all over the place. That creates instability, and not the kind you want. You want to be nice and tight: either be on your toes or get your feet flat, push your knees out, arch your back as you push your knees out, then take a breath through your nose and brace your core as hard as you can, squeeze your glutes, and that creates a solid foundation that will allow you to press heavier and heavier weight as you get better with the movement. As soon as you start to feel your core and lower body loosen up, reset yourself. Don’t start pressing repetitions with loose legs and a loose core. One quick bonus tip before I let you guys go: a lot of benches in the gym can be very

core and lower body loosen up, reset yourself. Don’t start pressing repetitions with loose legs and a loose core. One quick bonus tip before I let you guys go: a lot of benches in the gym can be very slippery. If your bench is slippery, your butt will slide up, your core will go soft, and you’ll lose your footing. You can take some bungees and wrap them around the seat, and your butt will stick to these really well. If you’re sliding on the bench, find a few bungees, put them on just like I did here, and that’s going to help you stick much better so that when you’re pressing heavier loads, your butt doesn’t start sliding all the way forward, which can become very dangerous, especially if you’re maxing out or trying to squeeze out those last few repetitions.
